items.q: Do fewer 'food miles' always mean a smaller environmental impact?
items.correct: Usually lower, but not always, since production methods and transport type also affect impact.
items.misconception: Food miles are the only thing that determines environmental impact.
items.random_wrong: Food miles measure the calories in food.
items.why: Students treat food miles as the sole environmental factor, ignoring how food is produced.

items.q: Does the Fairtrade mark guarantee the food is organic?
items.correct: No; Fairtrade is about fair prices and conditions for producers, not organic production.
items.misconception: Fairtrade and organic mean the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Fairtrade means the food is free.
items.why: Learners conflate two separate certification schemes with different aims.

items.q: What does 'seasonality' mean when choosing food?
items.correct: Eating foods when they are naturally harvested locally.
items.misconception: Buying any food that is currently on special offer.
items.random_wrong: Eating different foods on different days of the week.
items.why: Students confuse seasonality of harvest with promotions or routine variety.

items.correct: Distance food travels from production to consumption
items.why: It's Distance food travels from production to consumption. “Price of food per kilogram” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Buying strawberries grown locally in summer rather than imported in winter follows the principle of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Seasonality. “Pasteurisation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The Fairtrade mark mainly guarantees that producers receive:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A fair, guaranteed price. “Free packaging” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Information about where and how a food was produced is known as its:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Provenance. “Provenance tax” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
